result:
I’m going to write about the thing that is always humming beneath the floorboards of every other subject: the private, largely unverifiable conviction that reality is made of questions we haven’t yet learned how to ask, and that every human system—science, art, government, small talk— is a provisional gloss on that incomprehensible raw code. The essay will drift through personal memory, pop culture, neuro-chemistry, the history of ventilation ducts, the future of public libraries, the smell of photocopied zines, the way the word “index” sounds like a small boat creaking. It will not stay in its lane because experience refuses lanes. It will be 2500 words give or take, but words are only container ships; the cargo is the silence between them.

*

When I was eight I believed the house I grew up in was breathing. Not in the poetic sense adults later supplied—“old houses sigh”—but literally: inhale at dusk, exhale at dawn. I spent months stationed in the upstairs hallway with my palm against the yellow plaster, waiting for thoracic expansion. I never felt it, yet the conviction persisted, the way you can’t feel the planet’s thousand-mile-an-hour spin but still trust the textbook. One night I opened every window and door in an attempt to give the house a tracheotomy. My mother, frantic at the January cold, demanded I close up “before the furnace explodes.” I remember thinking: so even the furnace has a lung. Even pressure gauges hyperventilate.

Years later I read that buildings in earthquake zones are designed to “breathe” with tectonic shifts—rubberized foundations, flexible joints—so maybe the kid version of me was reverse-engineering a metaphor that civil engineers treat as math. The house was inhaling my childhood, exhaling an adulthood I’d eventually have to move into, like those hermit crabs that queue up shell-to-shell to swap exoskeletons on YouTube. I bring this up because I have never figured out where private obsession stops and the structural syllabus of reality begins. The border is as porous as cheesecloth, except cheesecloth is actually pretty coarse, so the metaphor fails; maybe the border is more like a Möbius strip that you keep walking only to meet yourself coming back wearing someone else’s report card.

*

A friend once told me he measures the health of a city by how many layers of posters you can peel off a light-pole at the bus stop. If the paper shreds after one rip, civic life is thin; if you excavate five or six strata of band announcements, room-for-rent fliers, lost-cat pleas, the metropolis has epidermis. I have since tested this in every place I’ve visited. The record so far is Thessaloniki, summer of 2019: twelve laminae, the bottom one advertising a 1985 concert of Mikis Theodorakis, which meant that paper had survived every Greek government swap, every euro crisis, every cloud of tear gas. I stood there peeling like an archaeologist of the immediate past, commuters streaming by, and felt I was holding a geological core sample. When I finally looked up, a teenage girl was watching me. She said in English, “You know you can just take a picture.” I laughed, but later I wondered whether taking a picture would have compressed the experience into a postcard of forgetting, whereas the slow unlayering let duration stay physical. Maybe memory is a form of litter we haven’t fully thrown away yet.

*

Neuroscientists now speak of “event boundaries”—moments when the brain stamps the word END on one experience and boots up a new file. Walking through a doorway is the textbook example: people lose the thread of what they came for because the hippocampus just hit save. I like to imagine those doorframes as tiny editors, red-penciling the manuscript of continuity. But what if the larger world is riddled with thresholds we don’t notice? Elevators, yes, but also changing Spotify tracks, switching from daylight to tungsten bulbs, smelling someone else’s laundry detergent in the laundromat. Each transition is a miniature death and rebirth, a Buddhist bureaucracy stamping visas on your consciousness so fast you think you’re a single person throughout the day. Maybe I keep losing my keys because I walked through three genre changes without filling out the customs forms.

*

Pop culture keeps rebooting the same stories because it intuits that we are all trapped in a remake of something, but the joke is we can’t locate the original. My parents swear the seventies smelled of newspapers left in rain; my grandparents swear the forties tasted of margarine and tin. I swear the early 2000s tasted of plastic lunchboxes and dial-up modem static. Each decade is a fan-edit that believes itself canonical. I remember watching “The Matrix” in 1999 and thinking déjà vu was a glitch in reality rather than a lag between short- and long-term memory; now every social feed repeats that déjà vu algorithmically—same meme, new font. We are living in a palimpsest where even the erasures have erasures. Yet the body refuses to be fooled: heart still jackhammers at genuine risk, pupils still dilate at the unexpected curve of a stranger’s collarbone. The wetware insists on first editions.

*

When I was writing my first novel I kept a Post-it above the desk that read: “The scene is not about what the scene is about.” I stole it from some forgotten craft podcast, but it became my fractal mantra. A couple arguing over washing dishes is arguing over the idea that time itself might be leaking from their marriage. A kid burying a dead beetle is hollowing out a grave for his future divorces. The miracle is that fiction can smuggle this surplus meaning under the guise of somebody opening the fridge. Reality, too, must abide by that rule: the glance you exchange with the driver in the opposite lane is freighted with unspoken bargains about mortality, but you both pretend it’s just a courtesy wave. Novels are merely the places where we practice noticing the subbasements; then, if we’re lucky, we remember to look for them when the book is closed.

*

I once read that the largest migration of mammals on earth happens not on the Serengeti but in the air above our heads: every night billions of insects ride high-altitude jet streams, an aerial river of biomass pulsing six kilometers up. We don’t notice because the swimmers are smaller than raindrops and the river is dark. I keep wondering what it feels like to be an ant caught in that flood, tumbling at a hundred kilometers an hour, past the blinking lights of passenger jets. Do their exoskeletons hum? Do they dream of branches? The thought is consolation: when my own life feels stalled, I remember that somewhere above an oceanic surge of invertebrates is commuting without passport or ticket, proving that movement doesn’t always require visibility. Progress might be a metric invented by creatures too obsessed with horizons to realize they’re already inside the sky.

*

The pandemic taught entire populations how to fear air. We masked, we HEPA-filtered, we held our breath on elevators. For a while I kept a small anemometer on my desk to measure the airflow in my apartment the way day-traders watch the NASDAQ. Below five cubic feet per minute I felt safe; above thirty, I imagined viral paratroopers landing on my cereal. The irony is that every exhalation is also a letter to the world: here is the taste of my coffee, the ghost of toothpaste, the carbon I borrowed from burning stars. To breathe is to Facebook-status-update the cosmos. I remember standing on an empty bridge in April 2020, looking at the spring trees, and realizing they were inhaling my exhaled wariness, spinning it into chlorophyll, into wood, into the idea of a future picnic. The transaction felt intimate, almost indecent, like swapping underwear with a stranger. Yet it’s been happening for four billion years; only the paperwork had changed.

*

Libraries have started lending out tools: sewing machines, thermal cameras, soil testers, ukuleles. I love the implicit admission that knowledge is a kind of lumber you sometimes need to clamp into shape with physical objects. One branch in my city has a “Library of Things” catalogue that includes a happiness lamp for seasonal affective disorder. You check it out for three weeks, let it pour fake sunlight on your retina, then return it for the next sad citizen. I picture the lamp accumulating layers of anonymous melancholy, like those posters in Thessaloniki, until it hums with the composite ghosts of winter after winter. Eventually the bulb won’t know whose blues it is dispelling; it just burns with the averaged tint of an entire zip code.

*

In high-school physics I learned that the universe is expanding because galaxies are red-shifting, their light stretching like taffy. The teacher said, “Imagine a raisin in rising dough; every raisin sees every other raisin moving away.” I raised my hand and asked, “What’s the dough made of?” He answered, “Spacetime itself,” but that only shoveled the mystery one layer deeper. Now when I feel my relationships drifting—old friends in other time zones, parents on the far side of cognitive decline—I picture us as raisins, and the dough is made of everything we haven’t said, expanding at 70 kilometers per second per megaparsec, which is a unit of distance so vast it sounds like a made-up spell. The only way to counteract the expansion is to introduce new mass: shared jokes, surprise postcards, Zoom funerals where nobody knows whether to wave or bow. Gravity is memory’s job security.

*

The word “nostalgia” was coined in 1688 by a Swiss doctor who thought it was a disease of demobilized soldiers; symptoms included fever, insomnia, and “a desire to return home that cannot be cured by any argument.” He prescribed mountain air and leeches. Today we prescribe streaming services. Yet the pathology persists: we are homesick not just for places but for the way time tasted when we didn’t yet know it was passing. I feel it most acutely in the cereal aisle, looking at boxes whose mascots have been redrawn in thinner, shinier lines. Somewhere inside me a seven-year-old is still convinced breakfast is an intergalactic fuel, and cartoons on the back are star maps. The adult me buys oat bran and reads the fiber count like a mortgage application. The distance between those two selves is the true expanding universe; no telescope will ever close it.

*

I used to think the opposite of depression was happiness, then I read that it’s actually vitality—the capacity to experience surprise. Depressed people predict tomorrow perfectly; the brain has calcified into a script. The cure, according to one experiment, is random compliments from strangers. Subjects who received text messages saying things like “Your eyebrows are mapmaking across your forehead” began to forecast differently; possibility leaked back in. I tried this by writing encouraging notes on Post-its and tucking them into returned library books. “You look like someone who hums lullabies to plants.” “Your late-night anxiety is a national monument.” I have no idea if anyone believed them, but imagining the moment a stranger finds improbable praise in a biography of Frida Kahlo keeps me awake in a good way, like plugging a nightlight into my own chest.

*

Ventilation ducts in office towers are large enough to crawl through if you weigh less than a ream of paper and have no personal boundary with dust. Hollywood exploits this for heist scenes, but the real magic is that they constitute a parallel city above the ceiling tiles, a labyrinth of galvanized steel where echoes lose their accents. Maintenance workers call it “the attic highway.” I picture memos and quarterly reports rising as invisible vapor, mixing with the ghosts of birthday balloons that popped in cubicles below. Over decades the ducts accumulate a sediment of exhaled slang: “synergy,” “paradigm shift,” “new normal,” each term going out of fashion but lingering as lint. Eventually someone replaces the ductwork and finds strata of buzzwords like the La Brea Tar Pits: “leverage” stuck to “optimize,” teeth sunk into the soft neck of “iterate.”

*

The future of public libraries, according to one futurist, is to become “third spaces” where algorithms can’t reach you. No surveillance cameras, no RFID tags, just paper and communal silence. Patrons will come to forget the way Big Data profiles them, to sit at big wooden tables that smell like lemon oil and read entire novels without a pop-up asking “Would you like to continue listening?” The librarian, retrained as a secular priest, will offer optional rites: a three-minute steganography workshop, a sermon on the semicolon. Checking out will require stamping a due-date card, the small thump of ink a ritual against the techno sublime. Kids will queue for the pleasure of hearing the stamp hit cardboard the way previous generations queued to flip cassette tapes. Identity will revert to handwriting on an envelope; anonymity will be the new celebrity.

*

I once tried to keep a diary of smells. Every night I wrote the most prominent olfactory memory of the day: chlorine from the gym, burnt cumin from a food truck, the sweet copper of my own blood after I bit my cheek. The problem is that language isn’t nasal; words about smell are metaphors borrowed from other senses. After a month the diary read like surrealist erotica: “This afternoon: the color beige wearing a leather jacket.” I gave up, but months later I found the notebook and experienced an uncanny time-travel; the synesthetic phrases acted like trapdoors back into the day. Smell may be the most democratic sense—everyone carries a portable archive—but its captions are written in disappearing ink. The diary sits in a drawer now, a seed bank of invisible flowers.

*

The word “index” comes from Latin “dicare,” to point, as in index finger. When I was small I thought the index of a book was a row of tiny doorways, each page number a portal you could slip through if you pressed hard enough with your finger. I still read indexes first, scanning for topics the author was too polite to foreground. The best ones contain rebellious Easter eggs: “existential dread, see also: laundry, page 127.” An index is a map drawn after the city has already been built, a confession that the thing you just spent 300 pages saying refuses linearity. I want my life to have an index: regret (discussed under: neon lights, dental work, 1998), awe (cross-referenced with cereal aisles, meteor showers, strangers who wink). When I die someone will close the back cover and flip here, looking for loopholes.

*

People think the speed of light is a limit, but the real speed limit is the slowness with which we update our opinion of someone. Neuroscience calls it “perseverance bias”; we form a snapshot and refuse to retake it even when the person has shaved their head or come out or survived cancer. I keep thinking about that when I read news stories that describe a nineteen-year-old who committed a crime as if he were a finished novella. We are all drafts in progress, committing plot twists offstage. The kindest thing you can do is gossip about someone as if you were pitching their future rather than summarizing their past. “He’s in the chapter where he hasn’t yet learned how forgiveness is a kind of time travel.” That’s how you bend spacetime: by letting others change faster than your resentment red-shifts.

*

Musicologists have recorded that the average tempo of pop hits has dropped 20 percent since the 1980s. We are steadily approaching a planetary resting heart rate. Soon lullabies will need to be remixed with dubstep just to stay awake. I joke, but I also wonder whether rhythm is a covert form of crowd control. Dictators ban drums for a reason; heartbeat sync is a gateway drug to empathy. Conversely, a slow song can anesthetize an entire elevator into politeness. The first time I heard “Blade Runner Blues” by Vangelis I was in a grocery store choosing bananas. By minute three I felt the absurdity of selecting fruit while a synth mimicked the loneliness of androids. I abandoned the cart and walked out into the parking lot, where dusk was doing something impossible with violet. The bananas stayed behind like hostages. I never ate them; I like to think they ripened into a small civilization that toppled and composted and fed the flowers someone else bought for a prom date.

*

We are told to “live in the present,” but physics says the present is a psychological illusion, a necessary user interface like the desktop trashcan on a computer—nowhere inside the circuitry will you find a tiny metal bucket. What we call “now” is the brain stitching a few milliseconds of sensory data into a story so we don’t trip over our own feet. I tried once to outwit the stitch by snapping my fingers exactly at the moment I perceived the snap. I couldn’t; the sound always arrived late, like gossip about myself. After weeks of trying I succeeded by accident, but the success felt indistinguishable from failure, because the snap dissolved into a cloud of anticipation. The present, it turns out, is a party that happens next door; you hear the laughter but never get the address.

*

One summer I worked at a plant nursery. The owner, a woman named Lila, could diagnose root rot by the way a customer said “Hello.” She told me plants recognize the sound of caterpillars chewing and release defensive chemicals only when they hear that specific vibration. She kept a tiny speaker hidden in the greenhouse that played looped munching sounds, and the tomatoes grew sturdier, tricked into arming themselves. I asked if the plants ever learned the ruse. She shrugged: “Probably, but by then it’s harvest season.” That’s when I understood that survival doesn’t require truth; it requires a story that works long enough. Politicians know this. So do parents splitting up who wait until the school year ends. We are all tomatoes with earbuds, ready to toughen up at the sound of imagined jaws.

*

There is a museum in Croatia that displays broken relationships. People donate objects: an ax used to chop up an ex’s furniture, a tin lunchbox never returned. Each item sits under glass with a placard telling its mini memoir. The curator says the most common post-visit behavior is for couples to hold hands tighter, as if the museum were a vaccination against future breakage. I wonder what an inverse museum would look like—one dedicated to relationships that never happened. A blank postcard you almost mailed, a phone number you almost called. The placards would be haiku of speculation. Visitors would leave lonelier but also larger, the way the night sky is bigger for the stars that burned out before we could see them.

*

They have begun 3D-printing villages for the homeless in Texas: houses the size of a hotel room, assembled in a day from concrete that looks like bone left out in moonlight. Critics complain the solution is “sterile,” that homelessness is structural not architectural, but I read an interview with a new resident who said the first night she slept inside printed walls she dreamed in paragraphs instead of panic. That seems like success enough. We keep forgetting that dignity is a substrate; once it sets, other miracles can germinate. The body has to feel safe before the mind can afford curiosity. I picture kids born in those printed homes growing up to regard “concrete” as a synonym for beginning, the way I once thought “asphalt” meant summer.

*

Astronomers say the Andromeda galaxy will collide with ours in four billion years. By then the sun will have swollen into a red giant, possibly engulfing Earth. I keep this fact in my pocket like a membership card for temporary troubles—argument at work, rent hike, dentist’s verdict. Yet the image that stays with me is not fiery apocalypse but gravitational reshuffling: two galaxies passing through each other like ghosts, stars flung into new choreographies, constellations unmade overnight. It’s the cosmic version of moving to a new city where nobody knows you used to stutter. The heavens will get a second draft, and whatever counts as eyes by then will look up and invent fresh myths about the shape of a swan that no longer exists. I find that comforting, like hearing that the library of unwritten books will be granted infinite shelves in a annex slated for demolition.

*

I return often to the memory of the house I thought breathed. If I stand very still inside the adult present, I can almost feel the inhale: yesterday’s headlines sliding under the baseboards, the dog’s bark echoing in the furnace. The exhale arrives at dawn, warm and smelling faintly of coffee grounds and the previous century. The house did not need lungs; it had us. We were the alveoli, opening and closing, trading stories for atmosphere, believing we lived inside the world when really the world lived inside our telling.

When I was small I thought the index of a book was a row of tiny doorways, each page number a portal you could slip through if you pressed hard enough with your finger. I still read indexes first, scanning for topics the author was too polite to foreground. The best ones contain rebellious Easter eggs: “existential dread, see also: laundry, page 127.” An index is a map drawn after the city has already been built, a confession that the thing you just spent 300 pages saying refuses linearity. I want my life to have an index: regret (discussed under: neon lights, dental work, 1998), awe (cross-referenced with cereal aisles, meteor showers, strangers who wink). When I die someone will close the back cover and flip here, looking for loopholes.

*

People think the speed of light is a limit, but the real speed limit is the slowness with which we update our opinion of someone. Neuroscience calls it “perseverance bias”; we form a snapshot and refuse to retake it even when the person has shaved their head or come out or survived cancer. I keep thinking about that when I read news stories that describe a nineteen-year-old who committed a crime as if he were a finished novella. We are all drafts in progress, committing plot twists offstage. The kindest thing you can do is gossip about someone as if you were pitching their future rather than summarizing their past. “He’s in the chapter where he hasn’t yet learned how forgiveness is a kind of time travel.” That’s how you bend spacetime: by letting others change faster than your resentment red-shifts.

*

Musicologists have recorded that the average tempo of pop hits has dropped 20 percent since the 1980s. We are steadily approaching a planetary resting heart rate. Soon lullabies will need to be remixed with dubstep just to stay awake. I joke, but I also wonder whether rhythm is a covert form of crowd control. Dictators ban drums for a reason; heartbeat sync is a gateway drug to empathy. Conversely, a slow song can anesthetize an entire elevator into politeness. The first time I heard “Blade Runner Blues” by Vangelis I was in a grocery store choosing bananas. By minute three I felt the absurdity of selecting fruit while a synth mimicked the loneliness of androids. I abandoned the cart and walked out into the parking lot, where dusk was doing something impossible with violet. The bananas stayed behind like hostages. I never ate them; I like to think they ripened into a small civilization that toppled and composted and fed the flowers someone else bought for a prom date.

*

We are told to “live in the present,” but physics says the present is a psychological illusion, a necessary user interface like the desktop trashcan on a computer—nowhere inside the circuitry will you find a tiny metal bucket. What we call “now” is the brain stitching a few milliseconds of sensory data into a story so we don’t trip over our own feet. I tried once to outwit the stitch by snapping my fingers exactly at the moment I perceived the snap. I couldn’t; the sound always arrived late, like gossip about myself. After weeks of trying I succeeded by accident, but the success felt indistinguishable from failure, because the snap dissolved into a cloud of anticipation. The present, it turns out, is a party that happens next door; you hear the laughter but never get the address.

*

One summer I worked at a plant nursery. The owner, a woman named Lila, could diagnose root rot by the way a customer said “Hello.” She told me plants recognize the sound of caterpillars chewing and release defensive chemicals only when they hear that specific vibration. She kept a tiny speaker hidden in the greenhouse that played looped munching sounds, and the tomatoes grew sturdier, tricked into arming themselves. I asked if the plants ever learned the ruse. She shrugged: “Probably, but by then it’s harvest season.” That’s when I understood that survival doesn’t require truth; it requires a story that works long enough. Politicians know this. So do parents splitting up who wait until the school year ends. We are all tomatoes with earbuds, ready to toughen up at the sound of imagined jaws.

*

There is a museum in Croatia that displays broken relationships. People donate objects: an ax used to chop up an ex’s furniture, a tin lunchbox never returned. Each item sits under glass with a placard telling its mini memoir. The curator says the most common post-visit behavior is for couples to hold hands tighter, as if the museum were a vaccination against future breakage. I wonder what an inverse museum would look like—one dedicated to relationships that never happened. A blank postcard you almost mailed, a phone number you almost called. The placards would be haiku of speculation. Visitors would leave lonelier but also larger, the way the night sky is bigger for the stars that burned out before we could see them.

*

They have begun 3D-printing villages for the homeless in Texas: houses the size of a hotel room, assembled in a day from concrete that looks like bone left out in moonlight. Critics complain the solution is “sterile,” that homelessness is structural not architectural, but I read an interview with a new resident who said the first night she slept inside printed walls she dreamed in paragraphs instead of panic. That seems like success enough. We keep forgetting that dignity is a substrate; once it sets, other miracles can germinate. The body has to feel safe before the mind can afford curiosity. I picture kids born in those printed homes growing up to regard “concrete” as a synonym for beginning, the way I once thought “asphalt” meant summer.

*

Astronomers say the Andromeda galaxy will collide with ours in four billion years. By then the sun will have swollen into a red giant, possibly engulfing Earth. I keep this fact in my pocket like a membership card for temporary troubles—argument at work, rent hike, dentist’s verdict. Yet the image that stays with me is not fiery apocalypse but gravitational reshuffling: two galaxies passing through each other like ghosts, stars flung into new choreographies, constellations unmade overnight. It’s the cosmic version of moving to a new city where nobody knows you used to stutter. The heavens will get a second draft, and whatever counts as eyes by then will look up and invent fresh myths about the shape of a swan that no longer exists. I find that comforting, like hearing that the library of unwritten books will be granted infinite shelves in a annex slated for demolition.

*

I return often to the memory of the house I thought breathed. If I stand very still inside the adult present, I can almost feel the inhale: yesterday’s headlines sliding under the baseboards, the dog’s bark echoing in the furnace. The exhale arrives at dawn, warm and smelling faintly of coffee grounds and the previous century. The house did not need lungs; it had us. We were the alveoli, opening and closing, trading stories for atmosphere, believing we lived inside the world when really the world lived inside our telling.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
